IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—knowledge base completion—by introducing ITransF, an interpretable embedding model that uses sparse attention to discover shared concepts and transfer statistical strength across relations. Empirical improvements on standard benchmarks (WN18 and FB15k) over many established baselines demonstrate practical value, and the learned sparse relation–concept associations offer clear interpretability that could change how researchers design transferable KBC models. While limitations on rare relations and some implementation constraints are noted, the approach is likely to inspire follow-up work and be adopted or extended by others in the area. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem (knowledge base completion) and proposes ITransF, an interpretable embedding model that uses sparse attention to discover shared concepts and transfer statistical strength across relations. It demonstrates empirical improvements in mean rank and Hits@10 on standard datasets (WN18, FB15k) over many baselines, suggesting practical value and likely adoption. The interpretable sparse attention mechanism opens meaningful research directions around concept sharing and interpretability in KBC, though impact may be limited by issues like data sparsity for rare relations and evaluation on only WN18/FB15k.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper’s topic—interpretable knowledge transfer for knowledge base completion—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues given active interest in knowledge graph embeddings and interpretability. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards: it uses standard KBC datasets (WN18, FB15k), reports common metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10), and compares against a comprehensive set of baselines (TransE, TransR, DISTMULT, STransE, etc.). The problem formulation (sparse attention over shared concept/projection matrices) and positioning (interpretable transfer learning for relations) target the right audience and research community for a top-tier conference. | B(5): The paper addresses knowledge base completion using embedding models and interpretable sparse attention, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences focused on representation learning and knowledge graphs. It targets the right audience by proposing a modeling contribution (ITransF) and comparing against a broad set of standard baselines (TransE, TransR, DISTMULT, etc.) using established KBC benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) and metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10). The problem formulation, use of sparse attention to share concept/projection matrices, and evaluation protocol match expectations for this venue. Its positioning and related-work coverage appear appropriate for the NLP/ML community studying knowledge graph embeddings and interpretability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly new embedding approach (ITransF) that uses a sparse attention mechanism to discover shared concept/projection matrices and transfer statistical strength across relations, which is a distinct perspective compared to listed baselines (e.g., TransE/TransR/STransE). The enforcement of L0-style sparsity (after L1 failure) to produce interpretable relation-to-concept associations is a non-trivial technical choice that differentiates it from prior work. While knowledge base completion is a well-studied problem, the interpretable sparse attention and concept-sharing formulation represent a notable and distinguishable novelty rather than a mere combination of existing techniques. | B(4): The paper proposes a distinct mechanism—sparse attention over a shared set of concept/projection matrices—to represent relations, which differs from prior per-relation projection methods (e.g., TransR, STransE) by enabling concept sharing and interpretable sparse associations. The use of enforced L0 constraints (after L1 failed) to obtain sparsity and interpretability is a specific technical choice that materially differentiates the method. Given the extensive baseline comparison and the described improvements, this represents a notable, non-trivial extension rather than a purely incremental combination of known techniques, and would likely be informative to experts in knowledge-graph embedding.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs evaluations on standard benchmarks (WN18 and FB15k) reporting mean rank and Hits@10 and compares to a wide range of baselines, which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. Training details (initialization from TransE, minibatch SGD, Bernoulli negative sampling) and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, and several limitations (data sparsity for rare relations, L1 regularization failure) are discussed. Missing, based on the summary, are explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the model components and attention sparsity mechanism, and broader dataset coverage (e.g., filtered/held-out or more robust variants) or code release was not mentioned. | B(3): The paper evaluates ITransF on standard benchmarks (WN18 and FB15k) and reports improvements in mean rank and Hits@10 against a broad set of baselines, and hyperparameters are reported—supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not mention any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the sparse attention design, only two datasets are used, and code is not mentioned, which limits assessment of robustness and reproducibility. It is positive that some limitations are acknowledged (rare-relation data sparsity; failed L1 regularization) but the experimental analysis appears adequate yet missing important deeper analyses.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions (ITransF and its sparse attention mechanism for concept sharing) are stated clearly early, with a coherent method section describing model initialization, training, and datasets (WN18, FB15k). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage is thorough, which together aid clarity and reproducibility. Minor presentation gaps remain: the summary does not mention figures/tables or released code, and while limitations are acknowledged, more explicit examples or visualizations of the interpretable attention vectors would improve clarity further. | B(4): The summary indicates the paper is well organized (motivating intro, clear methodology using sparse attention, and experiments on WN18/FB15k with standard metrics), and the main contributions are stated early (ITransF and its interpretable sparse-attention concept sharing). Notation and methodological description appear clearly defined enough to describe initialization, training, and constraints, and related work coverage is thorough; hyperparameters are reported and comparisons use many baselines, suggesting effective experimental presentation. The writing quality is noted as good, so overall presentation is strong though the summary does not explicitly confirm the quality/effectiveness of figures and tables, so I reserve the top score for minor presentation uncertainties.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against a large and relevant set of prior models (SE, TransE, TransH, TransR, CTransR, TransD, KG2E, DISTMULT, STransE, NTN, TATEC, PTransE, NLFeat, IRN, etc.), indicating comprehensive baseline coverage. Comparisons use the standard datasets (WN18, FB15k) and common metrics (mean rank, Hits@10), and improvements are reported specifically versus baselines that do not use additional information. The paper clearly analyzes differences (sparse attention, L0 constraint vs failed L1 attempts, relation-to-concept sharing) and the related work coverage is described as thorough, supporting fair and comprehensive comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares against a wide range of appropriate baselines (SE, TransE, TransH, TransR, CTransR, KG2E, TransD, TATEC, NTN, DISTMULT, STransE, RTransE, PTransE, NLFeat, Random Walk, IRN) and evaluates on standard datasets (WN18, FB15k) with common metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10), indicating fair experimental conditions and thorough related-work coverage. The authors also analyze methodological differences (sparse attention vs. L1 regularization and GPU-unfriendly sorting approaches) and discuss limitations like data sparsity. The summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgment when prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons are strong but miss that explicit discussion.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and are supported by reported improvements on mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k against a wide set of proper baselines. The mathematical/formulation aspects appear plausible from the summary, and the experimental setup uses standard datasets, metrics, and many relevant baselines, so the methodology is largely sound. Minor technical concerns remain: the paper does not report statistical significance or multiple-run variance, and the summary omits details about how the non‑differentiable L0 sparsity constraint is optimized and whether results are reproducible (code not mentioned), but these do not appear to undermine the main conclusions. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported: the paper proposes ITransF with sparse attention, describes model formulation and training (TransE init, minibatch SGD, Bernoulli negative sampling), and reports improvements on mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k against a comprehensive set of baselines. Mathematical and implementation choices (e.g. enforcing L0 constraints after L1 failure, and avoiding GPU-unfriendly sorting) are noted, but the summary does not report derivations or details of the L0 optimization procedure or any statistical significance testing of results. Overall the methodology and comparisons seem sound, with only minor technical details about sparse-constraint optimization and statistical evaluation left insufficiently documented in the provided summary.

main_claims: Proposes ITransF, a novel interpretable embedding model for knowledge base completion.
ITransF uses a sparse attention mechanism to discover hidden concepts of relations and transfer statistical strength by sharing concepts across relations.
The learned associations between relations and concepts are represented by sparse attention vectors that are easily interpretable.
ITransF achieves improvements on mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k over baselines that do not use additional information.
_summary.methodology: ITransF is an embedding model for KBC that employs a sparse attention mechanism to represent relations as sparse combinations of shared concept/projection matrices, with attention vectors linking relations to concepts; models are initialized from TransE, trained with minibatch SGD and Bernoulli negative sampling.
limitations: Data sparsity for rare relations (not all relations have abundant data)
Directly applying L1 regularization on attention vectors failed to produce sparse representations, motivating enforcing L0 constraints
The sorting operation used in several prior sparse-attention methods is not GPU-friendly
_summary.topic_area: Knowledge base completion / Knowledge graph embeddings
